seed: I feel lonely even when I'm around people, and then I get annoyed at myself for feeling that way.

transcript.content: I feel lonely even when I'm around people, and then I get annoyed at myself for feeling that way.

It sounds incredibly frustrating to experience that sense of loneliness despite being in a social setting, and to then feel annoyed with yourself for having those feelings. It’s understandable that this creates a difficult cycle. Perhaps we could explore what it’s like for you to be around others – what specifically do you notice about the situation that might be contributing to this feeling of loneliness?

Yeah, it is frustrating. Sometimes I just feel like I'm there but not really… connected, you know? I watch everyone else laughing or having a good time, and it’s like I’m just on the outside looking in. I guess I notice I keep thinking about how they all seem to be bonding and I'm just… missing something. It makes me feel even more isolated, like I'm not really

It sounds incredibly painful to experience that sense of being on the periphery, observing joy and connection without feeling like you’re part of it. It’s understandable that those thoughts would intensify the feeling of isolation you’re describing. Perhaps we could explore what it’s like to be in that moment, observing others, and what you’re noticing about yourself within that experience?

I don’t know, it’s just hard sometimes. I feel like I’m there, but not really part of things. Everyone’s laughing and having fun, and I just feel like I’m standing off to the side, watching it all happen without me. It’s kind of a lonely feeling, like I'm missing something that everyone else gets.

It sounds incredibly painful to experience that disconnect when you’re around others enjoying themselves. That feeling of being on the outside looking in, observing without truly participating, can really amplify those feelings of loneliness and missing out. Perhaps we could explore what it’s like to be in those moments and what you notice about yourself when you feel that way?

Yeah, it really does hurt. When I’m in those moments, I kind of freeze up, you know? I can feel my heart racing a bit, and I just start overthinking everything—like, what do I say? What if they don’t care? It’s like this spiral, and then I just end up standing there, feeling really small and wishing I could just blend in.

It sounds incredibly distressing to experience that intense feeling of hurt and the physical sensations accompanying it. The way you describe yourself freezing up and then getting caught in a loop of worrying thoughts – it’s understandable that you’d feel so small in those moments. Perhaps we could explore what’s driving those particular thoughts about what others might think?

Yeah, it is distressing. I think part of it is feeling like I have to put on this mask, like I have to be someone I’m not to fit in. It’s like I’m constantly measuring up to some standard that I just can’t reach. I worry that if I show my true self, people will see me as weird or not worth getting to know. It’s a

It sounds incredibly exhausting to carry that weight of needing to be someone other than yourself to feel accepted. The thought of constantly striving for an unattainable standard, coupled with the worry about judgment, can understandably create a lot of distress. Perhaps we could explore what that standard looks like for you and how it’s influencing your interactions with others?

Yeah, it really is exhausting. Sometimes, I can’t even pinpoint what that standard is. It feels like this vague idea of being funnier or more outgoing, like I have to be this perfect version of myself. And when I don’t live up to that, I just get this heavy feeling, like I’m letting everyone down, or worse, that they’re judging me. It makes it

It sounds incredibly draining to carry this sense of needing to meet an unspoken standard. That feeling of striving for something vague, like being funnier or more outgoing, can create a lot of pressure. Perhaps we could explore what it’s like to experience that feeling of letting others down – what thoughts and emotions come up for you in those moments?

It really is draining. When I think about letting others down, I feel this mix of shame and fear. Like, there's this voice in my head telling me I'm not good enough or that I’m a disappointment. It makes me want to just retreat even more, pull back, and avoid situations altogether. The thought that I could be judged or that someone might think I’m boring just sticks with me

It sounds incredibly challenging to carry that weight of worry with you. It’s understandable that those intense feelings of shame and fear would naturally lead you to want to withdraw and protect yourself from potential judgment. Perhaps we could explore what’s driving those very strong reactions to the possibility of letting others down – what does it mean to you to be seen as a disappointment?

Yeah, it really is tough. I guess being seen as a disappointment makes me feel like I’m somehow not worthy of love or connection. It’s like I have this fear that if people see those parts of me, they won't want to stick around. I grew up feeling like I had to be perfect to be accepted, and now it feels like that’s just embedded in me. Like if I

It sounds incredibly painful to carry that weight of feeling like a disappointment, and it makes perfect sense that you’d connect it to a fear of losing connection. It’s understandable that growing up with such high expectations would create a sense of needing to be perfect – perhaps it’s a pattern you’ve been noticing in your life.

Yeah, it really is painful. It’s like I’m always chasing this idea of perfection, but I never quite catch it. And then I end up feeling even more isolated when I fall short. It’s like this cycle—trying to live up to those expectations, feeling the pressure, and then when I don’t measure up, it just reinforces that fear of being alone. Sometimes I wonder if

It sounds incredibly frustrating to be caught in this pattern, constantly striving for something unattainable and then experiencing such a sharp contrast with the reality of your experience. The feeling of isolation that arises when you don’t meet those standards seems to be a really difficult element of this cycle for you. Perhaps we could explore what those expectations are and where they originate?

Yeah, it is really frustrating. I think those expectations come from a mix of things—growing up, I always felt like I had to be the best at everything, especially in school and with friends. I guess there was this pressure from my family to excel, and it just stuck with me. So now, when I’m in social situations, I’m just constantly measuring myself against this impossible

It sounds incredibly draining to carry that weight of expectation with you. It makes perfect sense that these feelings would surface when you’re in social situations, given the long-standing influence of those early experiences. Perhaps we could explore what specifically about these situations triggers this intense self-evaluation?

Yeah, it really does feel heavy. I think when I’m around others, especially in a group, I start to overanalyze everything. Like, I'm constantly scanning the room, trying to gauge their reactions. If I don’t laugh at the right moment or feel like I’m contributing enough, I spiral into those thoughts about not being interesting or fun. It amplifies that fear of being judged
